original_abstract:
Long-horizon time series forecasting is a critical yet challenging task, often plagued by high computational costs and prediction volatility as the forecasting window increases. To address these limitations, we introduce NHITS (Neural Hierarchical Interpolation for Time Series), a novel architecture that enhances neural forecasting efficiency and accuracy. Our approach incorporates two primary innovations: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation. By utilizing sub-sampling layers, the model scales its receptive field while significantly reducing the memory footprint. Through hierarchical interpolation, the model decomposes the input signal into various frequency bands, sequentially assembling the forecast from components at different scales.

We provide a theoretical foundation demonstrating that hierarchical interpolation can effectively approximate infinitely long horizons under smoothness assumptions. Empirically, we evaluate NHITS on six large-scale benchmark datasets from the long-horizon literature. Our results show that NHITS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based architectures, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 20% in multivariate settings. Furthermore, NHITS is significantly more efficient, reducing computational time by up to 50 times compared to existing leading methods. The model provides an interpretable, non-linear decomposition of forecasts, making it a robust and parsimonious solution for large-scale predictive systems.
humanized_abstract:
Long-horizon time series forecasting is a crucial but very challenging task that typically suffers from high computation cost and high forecast horizon volatility. In this work, we introduce a new method called Neural Hierarchical Interpolation for Time Series (NHITS) that aims to enhance the neural forecasting efficiency and accuracy for long-horizon tasks. Two key ideas are employed: (i) multi-rate data sampling (by designing sub-sampling layers that scale the receptive field in accordance with a compressed representation), and (ii) hierarchical interpolation (by splitting the input signal into separate bands and learning the forecast at each stage by fusing components in different frequency bands.

Abstract—we develop a theory showing that under some smoothness conditions hierarchical interpolation can emulate arbitrarily long horizons. Experimental validation on six large-scale datasets from the long-horizon literature strongly supports the efficacy of our model—namely the Non-Linear Hierarchical Interpolation Tree Spreadsheet model, (NHITS). Notably, on average, multivariate accuracy improvements over state-of-the-art Transformer based forecasting models are in excess of 20% while computational time reductions are in the range of 50×. Our primary model provides an interpretable, non-linear, and highly sparse decomposition of the underlying forecasts, which strongly argues in favor of its simplicity and robustness relative to existing large-scale forecasting systems.

Abstract—we develop a theory showing that under some smoothness conditions hierarchical interpolation can emulate arbitrarily long horizons. document.input:
Long-horizon time series forecasting is a critical yet challenging task, often plagued by high computational costs and prediction volatility as the forecasting window increases. To address these limitations, we introduce NHITS (Neural Hierarchical Interpolation for Time Series), a novel architecture that enhances neural forecasting efficiency and accuracy. Our approach incorporates two primary innovations: multi-rate data sampling and hierarchical interpolation. By utilizing sub-sampling layers, the model scales its receptive field while significantly reducing the memory footprint. Through hierarchical interpolation, the model decomposes the input signal into various frequency bands, sequentially assembling the forecast from components at different scales.

We provide a theoretical foundation demonstrating that hierarchical interpolation can effectively approximate infinitely long horizons under smoothness assumptions. Empirically, we evaluate NHITS on six large-scale benchmark datasets from the long-horizon literature. Our results show that NHITS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art Transformer-based architectures, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 20% in multivariate settings. Furthermore, NHITS is significantly more efficient, reducing computational time by up to 50 times compared to existing leading methods. The model provides an interpretable, non-linear decomposition of forecasts, making it a robust and parsimonious solution for large-scale predictive systems.
